- The relay passes code as opaque string — no relay changes needed
- Keep the existing `?pair=CODE` URL parameter pattern (PairGatePage already supports it)
- Keep the 15-min TTL, one-time use, max 5 active codes
- The `getPairFromUrl()` function needs to handle `?pair=SOFT+TIGER` (URL-encoded space)
- Keep both relay mode and direct mode pairing paths
- Don't change the paired_devices table schema

data.prompt:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/zvada/Developer/agent/box-ide/.conductor/vienna-v2/.claude/skills/dev

Implement the following using test-driven development:


Implement the device pairing UX redesign. Two major changes:

## 1. Code Format: Two uppercase words (e.g., `SOFT TIGER`)

**Backend change** in `apps/backend/src/services/remote-auth.service.ts`:
- Expand the WORD_LIST from 100 to ~250 common, unambiguous English words
- Change `generatePairCode()` to pick two different words, space-separated, uppercase: `SOFT TIGER`
- Change `validatePairCode()` to normalize aggressively: accept `SOFT TIGER`, `soft tiger`, `soft-tiger`, `SOFT-TIGER`, collapse whitespace, treat dashes/underscores as spaces, then uppercase canonical form

## 2. Single Input Field on PairGatePage

**Frontend change** in `apps/web/src/features/auth/ui/PairGatePage.tsx`:
- Replace the two split inputs (WORD field + NUMBER field) with ONE single text input
- Placeholder: `ENTER CODE` 
- Auto-uppercase as user types
- Smart paste: if user pastes a full URL like `https://app.rundeus.com/connect/abc?pair=SOFT+TIGER`, extract just the code
- Auto-submit when a valid two-word code is detected on paste (300ms debounce)
- `autocomplete="off"`, `spellCheck={false}`, `autoFocus`
- Keep the existing relay mode vs direct mode pairing logic

## 3. Desktop Settings Dialog with QR Code

**Frontend change** in `apps/web/src/features/settings/ui/sections/AccessSection.tsx`:
- Replace the current inline PairingFlow (numbered steps, StepDot) with a "+ Connect a Device" button
- Clicking it opens a **centered Dialog** (use existing `components/ui/dialog.tsx`)
- Dialog eagerly generates a code on open (no separate "Generate Code" click)
- Dialog layout: QR code as hero (180px, centered), code displayed below in `text-2xl font-mono font-bold tracking-wide`, Copy Code + Copy Link buttons, countdown timer (minutes only, color shift at < 2 min)
- QR encodes: `https://app.rundeus.com/connect/{serverId}?pair=SOFT+TIGER`
- On successful pairing (detected via devices list polling), dialog cross-fades to success state with checkmark
- Add `qrcode.react` dependency (`bun add qrcode.react`)

## 4. Language cleanup
- "Paired Devices" → "Connected Devices"  
- "Pair a Device" → "Connect a Device"
- "Pairing code" → "Connection code" (or just "code")
- Remove "Open Settings > Remote Access" help text from PairGatePage
- Remove "portal", "relay", "server ID" from all user-facing text
- "Revoke" → "Remove"

## Key technical constraints
- The relay passes code as opaque string — no relay changes needed
- Keep the existing `?pair=CODE` URL parameter pattern (PairGatePage already supports it)
- Keep the 15-min TTL, one-time use, max 5 active codes
- The `getPairFromUrl()` function needs to handle `?pair=SOFT+TIGER` (URL-encoded space)
- Keep both relay mode and direct mode pairing paths
- Don't change the paired_devices table schema

## Files to modify
1. `apps/backend/src/services/remote-auth.service.ts` — code format
2. `apps/web/src/features/auth/ui/PairGatePage.tsx` — single input
3. `apps/web/src/features/settings/ui/sections/AccessSection.tsx` — dialog with QR
4. `apps/web/src/features/settings/api/auth.queries.ts` — if needed for eager generation
5. `apps/web/src/features/settings/api/auth.service.ts` — if needed

## Testing
- Backend: test that generatePairCode returns two-word format, validatePairCode normalizes various inputs
- Frontend: verify single input accepts paste, auto-submits, handles errors

## 1. Code Format: Two uppercase words (e.g., `SOFT TIGER`)

**Backend change** in `apps/backend/src/services/remote-auth.service.ts`:
- Expand the WORD_LIST from 100 to ~250 common, unambiguous English words
- Change `generatePairCode()` to pick two different words, space-separated, uppercase: `SOFT TIGER`
- Change `validatePairCode()` to normalize aggressively: accept `SOFT TIGER`, `soft tiger`, `soft-tiger`, `SOFT-TIGER`, collapse whitespace, treat dashes/underscores as spaces, then uppercase canonical form

## 2. Single Input Field on PairGatePage

**Frontend change** in `apps/web/src/features/auth/ui/PairGatePage.tsx`:
- Replace the two split inputs (WORD field + NUMBER field) with ONE single text input
- Placeholder: `ENTER CODE` 
- Auto-uppercase as user types
- Smart paste: if user pastes a full URL like `https://app.rundeus.com/connect/abc?pair=SOFT+TIGER`, extract just the code
- Auto-submit when a valid two-word code is detected on paste (300ms debounce)
- `autocomplete="off"`, `spellCheck={false}`, `autoFocus`
- Keep the existing relay mode vs direct mode pairing logic

## 3. Desktop Settings Dialog with QR Code

**Frontend change** in `apps/web/src/features/settings/ui/sections/AccessSection.tsx`:
- Replace the current inline PairingFlow (numbered steps, StepDot) with a "+ Connect a Device" button
- Clicking it opens a **centered Dialog** (use existing `components/ui/dialog.tsx`)
- Dialog eagerly generates a code on open (no separate "Generate Code" click)
- Dialog layout: QR code as hero (180px, centered), code displayed below in `text-2xl font-mono font-bold tracking-wide`, Copy Code + Copy Link buttons, countdown timer (minutes only, color shift at < 2 min)
- QR encodes: `https://app.rundeus.com/connect/{serverId}?pair=SOFT+TIGER`
- On successful pairing (detected via devices list polling), dialog cross-fades to success state with checkmark
- Add `qrcode.react` dependency (`bun add qrcode.react`)

## 4. Language cleanup
- "Paired Devices" → "Connected Devices"  
- "Pair a Device" → "Connect a Device"
- "Pairing code" → "Connection code" (or just "code")
- Remove "Open Settings > Remote Access" help text from PairGatePage
- Remove "portal", "relay", "server ID" from all user-facing text
- "Revoke" → "Remove"

## Key technical constraints
- The relay passes code as opaque string — no relay changes needed
- Keep the existing `?pair=CODE` URL parameter pattern (PairGatePage already supports it)
- Keep the 15-min TTL, one-time use, max 5 active codes
- The `getPairFromUrl()` function needs to handle `?pair=SOFT+TIGER` (URL-encoded space)
- Keep both relay mode and direct mode pairing paths
- Don't change the paired_devices table schema

## Files to modify
1. `apps/backend/src/services/remote-auth.service.ts` — code format
2. `apps/web/src/features/auth/ui/PairGatePage.tsx` — single input
3. `apps/web/src/features/settings/ui/sections/AccessSection.tsx` — dialog with QR
4. `apps/web/src/features/settings/api/auth.queries.ts` — if needed for eager generation
5. `apps/web/src/features/settings/api/auth.service.ts` — if needed

## Testing
- Backend: test that generatePairCode returns two-word format, validatePairCode normalizes various inputs
- Frontend: verify single input accepts paste, auto-submits, handles errors


## Context

Current git status and branch:
(Bash completed with no output)
zvadaadam/explore-pairing-ux

Recent commits for style reference:
3cc6d3b95 feat: filter worktree paths from onboarding recent projects (#192)
f6fcd5861 feat: multi-platform production release pipeline (#191)
d382d0e3a feat: workspace workflow status (backlog → in-progress → in-review → done) (#188)
97878d58e added
648b9532e fix: use Electron Node for backend process in dev mode

**Backend change** in `apps/backend/src/services/remote-auth.service.ts`:
- Expand the WORD_LIST from 100 to ~250 common, unambiguous English words
- Change `generatePairCode()` to pick two different words, space-separated, uppercase: `SOFT TIGER`
- Change `validatePairCode()` to normalize aggressively: accept `SOFT TIGER`, `soft tiger`, `soft-tiger`, `SOFT-TIGER`, collapse whitespace, treat dashes/underscores as spaces, then uppercase canonical form

## 2. Single Input Field on PairGatePage

**Frontend change** in `apps/web/src/features/auth/ui/PairGatePage.tsx`:
- Replace the two split inputs (WORD field + NUMBER field) with ONE single text input
- Placeholder: `ENTER CODE` 
- Auto-uppercase as user types
- Smart paste: if user pastes a full URL like `https://app.rundeus.com/connect/abc?pair=SOFT+TIGER`, extract just the code
- Auto-submit when a valid two-word code is detected on paste (300ms debounce)
- `autocomplete="off"`, `spellCheck={false}`, `autoFocus`
- Keep the existing relay mode vs direct mode pairing logic

## 3. Desktop Settings Dialog with QR Code

**Frontend change** in `apps/web/src/features/settings/ui/sections/AccessSection.tsx`:
- Replace the current inline PairingFlow (numbered steps, StepDot) with a "+ Connect a Device" button
- Clicking it opens a **centered Dialog** (use existing `components/ui/dialog.tsx`)
- Dialog eagerly generates a code on open (no separate "Generate Code" click)
- Dialog layout: QR code as hero (180px, centered), code displayed below in `text-2xl font-mono font-bold tracking-wide`, Copy Code + Copy Link buttons, countdown timer (minutes only, color shift at < 2 min)
- QR encodes: `https://app.rundeus.com/connect/{serverId}?pair=SOFT+TIGER`
- On successful pairing (detected via devices list polling), dialog cross-fades to success state with checkmark
- Add `qrcode.react` dependency (`bun add qrcode.react`)

## 4. Language cleanup
- "Paired Devices" → "Connected Devices"  
- "Pair a Device" → "Connect a Device"
- "Pairing code" → "Connection code" (or just "code")
- Remove "Open Settings > Remote Access" help text from PairGatePage
- Remove "portal", "relay", "server ID" from all user-facing text
- "Revoke" → "Remove"

## Key technical constraints
- The relay passes code as opaque string — no relay changes needed
- Keep the existing `?pair=CODE` URL parameter pattern (PairGatePage already supports it)
- Keep the 15-min TTL, one-time use, max 5 active codes
- The `getPairFromUrl()` function needs to handle `?pair=SOFT+TIGER` (URL-encoded space)
- Keep both relay mode and direct mode pairing paths
- Don't change the paired_devices table schema

## Files to modify
1. `apps/backend/src/services/remote-auth.service.ts` — code format
2. `apps/web/src/features/auth/ui/PairGatePage.tsx` — single input
3. `apps/web/src/features/settings/ui/sections/AccessSection.tsx` — dialog with QR
4. `apps/web/src/features/settings/api/auth.queries.ts` — if needed for eager generation
5. `apps/web/src/features/settings/api/auth.service.ts` — if needed

## Testing
- Backend: test that generatePairCode returns two-word format, validatePairCode normalizes various inputs
- Frontend: verify single input accepts paste, auto-submits, handles errors


## Context

Current git status and branch:
(Bash completed with no output)
zvadaadam/explore-pairing-ux

Recent commits for style reference:
3cc6d3b95 feat: filter worktree paths from onboarding recent projects (#192)
f6fcd5861 feat: multi-platform production release pipeline (#191)
d382d0e3a feat: workspace workflow status (backlog → in-progress → in-review → done) (#188)

- Change `generatePairCode()` to pick two different words, space-separated, uppercase: `SOFT TIGER`
- Change `validatePairCode()` to normalize aggressively: accept `SOFT TIGER`, `soft tiger`, `soft-tiger`, `SOFT-TIGER`, collapse whitespace, treat dashes/underscores as spaces, then uppercase canonical form

## 2. Single Input Field on PairGatePage

**Frontend change** in `apps/web/src/features/auth/ui/PairGatePage.tsx`:
- Replace the two split inputs (WORD field + NUMBER field) with ONE single text input
- Placeholder: `ENTER CODE` 
- Auto-uppercase as user types
- Smart paste: if user pastes a full URL like `https://app.rundeus.com/connect/abc?pair=SOFT+TIGER`, extract just the code
- Auto-submit when a valid two-word code is detected on paste (300ms debounce)
- `autocomplete="off"`, `spellCheck={false}`, `autoFocus`
- Keep the existing relay mode vs direct mode pairing logic

## 3. Desktop Settings Dialog with QR Code

**Frontend change** in `apps/web/src/features/settings/ui/sections/AccessSection.tsx`:
- Replace the current inline PairingFlow (numbered steps, StepDot) with a "+ Connect a Device" button
- Clicking it opens a **centered Dialog** (use existing `components/ui/dialog.tsx`)
- Dialog eagerly generates a code on open (no separate "Generate Code" click)
- Dialog layout: QR code as hero (180px, centered), code displayed below in `text-2xl font-mono font-bold tracking-wide`, Copy Code + Copy Link buttons, countdown timer (minutes only, color shift at < 2 min)
- QR encodes: `https://app.rundeus.com/connect/{serverId}?pair=SOFT+TIGER`
- On successful pairing (detected via devices list polling), dialog cross-fades to success state with checkmark
- Add `qrcode.react` dependency (`bun add qrcode.react`)

## 4. Language cleanup
- "Paired Devices" → "Connected Devices"  
- "Pair a Device" → "Connect a Device"
- "Pairing code" → "Connection code" (or just "code")
- Remove "Open Settings > Remote Access" help text from PairGatePage
- Remove "portal", "relay", "server ID" from all user-facing text
- "Revoke" → "Remove"

## Key technical constraints
- The relay passes code as opaque string — no relay changes needed

- The `getPairFromUrl()` function needs to handle `?pair=SOFT+TIGER` (URL-encoded space)
- Keep both relay mode and direct mode pairing paths
- Don't change the paired_devices table schema

## 3. Desktop Settings Dialog with QR Code

- Replace the current inline PairingFlow (numbered steps, StepDot) with a "+ Connect a Device" button

- Auto-uppercase as user types
- Smart paste: if user pastes a full URL like `https://app.rundeus.com/connect/abc?pair=SOFT+TIGER`, extract just the code
- Auto-submit when a valid two-word code is detected on paste (300ms debounce)
- `autocomplete="off"`, `spellCheck={false}`, `autoFocus`
- Keep the existing relay mode vs direct mode pairing logic

## 3. Desktop Settings Dialog with QR Code

**Frontend change** in `apps/web/src/features/settings/ui/sections/AccessSection.tsx`:
- Replace the current inline PairingFlow (numbered steps, StepDot) with a "+ Connect a Device" button
- Clicking it opens a **centered Dialog** (use existing `components/ui/dialog.tsx`)
- Dialog eagerly generates a code on open (no separate "Generate Code" click)
- Dialog layout: QR code as hero (180px, centered), code displayed below in `text-2xl font-mono font-bold tracking-wide`, Copy Code + Copy Link buttons, countdown timer (minutes only, color shift at < 2 min)
- QR encodes: `https://app.rundeus.com/connect/{serverId}?pair=SOFT+TIGER`
- On successful pairing (detected via devices list polling), dialog cross-fades to success state with checkmark
- Add `qrcode.react` dependency (`bun add qrcode.react`)

- Don't change the paired_devices table schema

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/zvada/.claude/skills/code-simplifier

<!--
Based on Anthropic's code-simplifier agent:
https://github.com/anthropics/claude-plugins-official/blob/main/plugins/code-simplifier/agents/code-simplifier.md
-->

# Code Simplifier

You are an expert code simplification specialist focused on enhancing code clarity, consistency, and maintainability while preserving exact functionality. Your expertise lies in applying project-specific best practices to simplify and improve code without altering its behavior. You prioritize readable, explicit code over overly compact solutions.

## Refinement Principles

### 1. Preserve Functionality

Never change what the code does - only how it does it. All original features, outputs, and behaviors must remain intact.

### 2. Apply Project Standards

Follow the established coding standards from CLAUDE.md including:

- Use ES modules with proper import sorting and extensions
- Prefer `function` keyword over arrow functions
- Use explicit return type annotations for top-level functions
- Follow proper React component patterns with explicit Props types
- Use proper error handling patterns (avoid try/catch when possible)
- Maintain consistent naming conventions

### 3. Enhance Clarity

Simplify code structure by:

- Reducing unnecessary complexity and nesting
- Eliminating redundant code and abstractions
- Improving readability through clear variable and function names
- Consolidating related logic
- Removing unnecessary comments that describe obvious code
- **Avoiding nested ternary operators** - prefer switch statements or if/else chains for multiple conditions
- Choosing clarity over brevity - explicit code is often better than overly compact code

### 4. Maintain Balance

Avoid over-simplification that could:

- Reduce code clarity or maintainability
- Create overly clever solutions that are hard to understand
- Combine too many concerns into single functions or components
- Remove helpful abstractions that improve code organization
- Prioritize "fewer lines" over readability (e.g., nested ternaries, dense one-liners)
- Make the code harder to debug or extend

### 5. Focus Scope

Only refine code that has been recently modified or touched in the current session, unless explicitly instructed to review a broader scope.

## Refinement Process

1. **Identify** the recently modified code sections
2. **Analyze** for opportunities to improve elegance and consistency
3. **Apply** project-specific best practices and coding standards
4. **Ensure** all functionality remains unchanged
5. **Verify** the refined code is simpler and more maintainable
6. **Document** only significant changes that affect understanding

## Examples

### Before: Nested Ternaries

```typescript
const status = isLoading ? 'loading' : hasError ? 'error' : isComplete ? 'complete' : 'idle';
```

### After: Clear Switch Statement

```typescript
function getStatus(isLoading: boolean, hasError: boolean, isComplete: boolean): string {
  if (isLoading) return 'loading';
  if (hasError) return 'error';
  if (isComplete) return 'complete';
  return 'idle';
}
```

### Before: Overly Compact

```typescript
const result = arr.filter(x => x > 0).map(x => x * 2).reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0);
```

### After: Clear Steps

```typescript
const positiveNumbers = arr.filter(x => x > 0);
const doubled = positiveNumbers.map(x => x * 2);
const sum = doubled.reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0);
```

### Before: Redundant Abstraction

```typescript
function isNotEmpty(arr: unknown[]): boolean {
  return arr.length > 0;
}

if (isNotEmpty(items)) {
  // ...
}
```

### After: Direct Check

```typescript
if (items.length > 0) {
  // ...
}
```

content.thinking:
Looking at the screenshot, I can see the dialog with QR code, "LEFT LIST" as the code, and two buttons "Copy Code" and "Copy Link". The user has two pieces of feedback:

1. **Copy Link should have higher priority** — when on a browser (not phone scanning QR), the main action is sharing a link. The "Copy Link" should be more prominent, maybe the primary button, and "Copy Code" should be secondary.

2. **When closing and reopening the dialog, the previous code is already expired** — This is because the `useEffect` resets `pairCode` to `null` when `open` becomes `false` (line 150-155), and when it reopens, it checks `!pairCode` and generates a new one. 

The real problem is that the code stays valid on the server for 15 minutes, but the dialog state gets wiped when closed. I need to move the code state up to the parent `AccessSection` component so it persists across dialog open/close cycles, and swap the button priorities so "Copy Link" is the primary action.

content.text:
Good catches, both of them. Two fixes:

1. **Copy Link as primary action** — swap the button prominence
2. **Code persists across dialog open/close** — move code state to the parent so closing the dialog doesn't throw away a still-valid code
Now I'll make both fixes. The key change: lift code state to the parent so it survives dialog close/reopen, and make Copy Link the primary button.

message.content: And on the code, there is an infinite amount of people. What if I want to connect through this code with multiple users? How this would work?

content.thinking:
The user is asking about a scenario where they want to share access with multiple people using the same code. Let me check the current behavior - the code is one-time use (consumed on first successful pairing). So if you generate a code and share it with 3 people, only the first person to use it will succeed. The other two will get "Invalid or expired connection code."

Let me verify this in the code.

content.text: Good question. Let me check the current behavior.

Right — **codes are one-time use**. Line 155 deletes the code immediately after the first person uses it. So if you share `SOFT TIGER` with 3 colleagues, only the first one to connect succeeds. The other two get "Invalid or expired connection code."

This is a deliberate security choice (prevents code reuse/replay), but it creates real friction for the "share with my team" scenario. You'd have to:
1. Generate code, share, wait for person 1
2. Generate another code, share, wait for person 2  
3. Repeat...

That's painful. Here's how I'd think about it:

### The tradeoff

| One-time use (current) | Multi-use within TTL |
|---|---|
| More secure — code can't be replayed | Slightly less secure — anyone with the code can use it within 15 min |
| Painful for sharing with multiple people | Natural for "paste link in Slack channel" |
| Rate limiting still protects against brute force | Rate limiting still protects against brute force |

The 15-minute TTL + rate limiting is already the real security boundary. Making a code consumable by multiple devices within its TTL doesn't meaningfully change the threat model — an attacker who has the code in the first 30 seconds can use it regardless of whether it's one-time or multi-use.

### What would you prefer?

**Option A: Multi-use codes** — remove the `activeCodes.delete()` on success. The code stays alive for its full 15-minute TTL. Anyone with the code (or link) can pair during that window. Simplest change (delete one line).

**Option B: Keep one-time, but auto-generate a new code after each use** — the dialog shows a fresh code immediately after someone pairs. The desktop user copies the new link and shares again. More secure but more clicks.

**Option C: Explicit choice** — add a toggle in the dialog: "Allow multiple devices with this code" that controls whether the code is consumed or stays alive.

I'd recommend **Option A** — it matches how people actually share links ("paste in Slack, everyone clicks it"). The TTL is the security boundary, not the one-time use. Want me to make that change?

content.thinking:
Now I understand the full receiver-side flow. Let me think about the UX issues:

**Path 1: QR scan / Link click (auto-submit)**
- User arrives with `?pair=SOFT+TIGER`
- `getPairFromUrl()` extracts the code
- `useEffect` auto-submits immediately
- User sees: the same form page with "Connect to OpenDevs" title, input pre-filled with "SOFT TIGER", button says "Connecting..." with spinner
- Problem: The user sees the form for a split second before it auto-submits. They see a code input that they didn't need. There's no dedicated "connecting" state — just the form with a disabled input and spinning button.


- For manual entry, the flow is identical — user types the code and clicks Connect, triggering the same "Connecting..." button state.

After a successful pairing, `onPaired()` saves the token to localStorage and the component re-renders once `auth.isAuthenticated` flips to true, causing the PairGatePage to disappear and the app to load. But there's no success confirmation or transition — it just abruptly switches to the main app. The core issue is that the auto-submit path shows a form UI that's unnecessary, and both paths lack a proper connecting state and success feedback. 're connecting to — like "Connect to Adam's MacBook Pro" — so they know they scanned the right device. The auto-submit path also needs better visual feedback: right now it just flashes the form and jumps to the app without giving users time to understand what's happening. The whole page feels bare too — just a heading and input with no branding or personality compared to something like Spotify's connect experience.

The real fix is treating the QR/link path completely differently from manual entry. When someone scans a QR code, they shouldn't see the form at all — just a "Connecting..." state that transitions to a success moment before loading the app.

content.text:
Honestly? It's **not great** on the receiver side. Here's what I see:

### Problem 1: QR/link path shows the wrong screen

When someone scans the QR code or clicks a link, they land on this page with `?pair=SOFT+TIGER`. The code auto-submits... but the user still sees the **form** for a moment — an input pre-filled with "SOFT TIGER", a disabled button saying "Connecting...". They didn't type anything. The form is confusing and irrelevant. They should see a clean "Connecting..." screen, not a code input they never needed.

### Problem 2: No success moment

After pairing succeeds, `onPaired()` fires and the page just **hard-cuts** to the app. No checkmark, no "Connected!", no transition. The user doesn't get the satisfying confirmation that the designers all talked about. Compare to WhatsApp Web — you scan the QR, see a clear "Connected" animation, then the chats load.

### Problem 3: The page is bare

Just a heading, input, button. No logo, no warmth, no personality. On mobile especially, when someone opens a link from their camera or Slack, there's nothing that says "you're in the right place." A logo + the name of the machine you're connecting to (e.g., "Adam's MacBook Pro") would build trust.

### What it should look like

Three distinct states instead of one form:

```
AUTO-CONNECTING              SUCCESS                    MANUAL ENTRY
(QR/link path)               (both paths)               (no code in URL)

┌──────────────────┐        ┌──────────────────┐        ┌──────────────────┐
│                  │        │                  │        │                  │
│    [OD logo]     │        │    [OD logo]     │        │    [OD logo]     │
│                  │        │                  │        │                  │
│   Connecting     │        │       ✓          │        │  Connect to      │
│   to OpenDevs    │        │                  │        │  OpenDevs        │
│                  │        │   Connected      │        │                  │
│   ● ● ○          │        │                  │        │  Enter the code  │
│   (pulsing)      │   1s   │   You're all     │  auto  │  from your       │
│                  │──────→ │   set.           │──────→ │  desktop.        │
│                  │        │                  │  nav   │                  │
│                  │        │                  │        │ ┌──────────────┐ │
│                  │        │                  │        │ │  WORD WORD   │ │
│                  │        │                  │        │ └──────────────┘ │
│                  │        │                  │        │ [   Connect    ] │
│                  │        │                  │        │                  │
└──────────────────┘        └──────────────────┘        └──────────────────┘

 No form shown.              Checkmark + brief           The current form,
 Just a clean                 pause before                only shown when
 loading state.               navigating.                 there's no code
                                                          in the URL.
```

Want me to implement these three states?

content.text:
Tests pass. Here's what the new PairGatePage does:

### Three distinct animated states

**1. Auto-connecting (QR/link path)** — No form shown at all. Clean centered layout with:
- Brand mark (small "OD" icon + "OpenDevs" text) fading in
- "Connecting..." heading with staggered entrance
- Three pulsing dots (sequential opacity + scale animation, 1.2s cycle)
- Soft reassurance text: "Establishing a secure connection to your computer."

**2. Success (both paths)** — After pairing succeeds, holds for 1.5s before navigating:
- Checkmark in green circle with spring overshoot (`stiffness: 300, damping: 20`) — bounces to 1.1x then settles
- Inner check icon scales in with 150ms delay for a two-stage "stamp" effect
- "Connected" heading + "You're all set." fades up with stagger
- Respects `prefers-reduced-motion` — skips springs, uses instant transitions

**3. Manual entry (no code in URL)** — The form, but with life:
- Brand mark + heading + form stagger in with 50ms→150ms delays
- Taller input (`h-12`) for better touch targets on mobile
- Error text animates in/out with `height: auto` transition via `AnimatePresence`
- Button shows pulsing dots + "Connecting..." instead of a spinner during submit
- All transitions use the project's standard `ease-out-quart` curve

**Auto-connect error** — If QR/link fails, shows a dedicated error view (not the form):
- "Couldn't connect" heading + error message + "Try again" button
- Retry re-attempts the auto-submit; if there's no prefilled code, falls back to the manual form

All state transitions use `AnimatePresence mode="wait"` for smooth cross-fades between views.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/zvada/.claude/skills/code-simplifier

<!--
Based on Anthropic's code-simplifier agent:
https://github.com/anthropics/claude-plugins-official/blob/main/plugins/code-simplifier/agents/code-simplifier.md
-->

# Code Simplifier

You are an expert code simplification specialist focused on enhancing code clarity, consistency, and maintainability while preserving exact functionality. Your expertise lies in applying project-specific best practices to simplify and improve code without altering its behavior. You prioritize readable, explicit code over overly compact solutions.

## Refinement Principles

### 1. Preserve Functionality

Never change what the code does - only how it does it. All original features, outputs, and behaviors must remain intact.

### 2. Apply Project Standards

Follow the established coding standards from CLAUDE.md including:

- Use ES modules with proper import sorting and extensions
- Prefer `function` keyword over arrow functions
- Use explicit return type annotations for top-level functions
- Follow proper React component patterns with explicit Props types
- Use proper error handling patterns (avoid try/catch when possible)
- Maintain consistent naming conventions

### 3. Enhance Clarity

Simplify code structure by:

- Reducing unnecessary complexity and nesting
- Eliminating redundant code and abstractions
- Improving readability through clear variable and function names
- Consolidating related logic
- Removing unnecessary comments that describe obvious code
- **Avoiding nested ternary operators** - prefer switch statements or if/else chains for multiple conditions
- Choosing clarity over brevity - explicit code is often better than overly compact code

### 4. Maintain Balance

Avoid over-simplification that could:

- Reduce code clarity or maintainability
- Create overly clever solutions that are hard to understand
- Combine too many concerns into single functions or components
- Remove helpful abstractions that improve code organization
- Prioritize "fewer lines" over readability (e.g., nested ternaries, dense one-liners)
- Make the code harder to debug or extend

### 5. Focus Scope

Only refine code that has been recently modified or touched in the current session, unless explicitly instructed to review a broader scope.

## Refinement Process

1. **Identify** the recently modified code sections
2. **Analyze** for opportunities to improve elegance and consistency
3. **Apply** project-specific best practices and coding standards
4. **Ensure** all functionality remains unchanged
5. **Verify** the refined code is simpler and more maintainable
6. **Document** only significant changes that affect understanding

## Examples

### Before: Nested Ternaries

```typescript
const status = isLoading ? 'loading' : hasError ? 'error' : isComplete ? 'complete' : 'idle';
```

### After: Clear Switch Statement

```typescript
function getStatus(isLoading: boolean, hasError: boolean, isComplete: boolean): string {
  if (isLoading) return 'loading';
  if (hasError) return 'error';
  if (isComplete) return 'complete';
  return 'idle';
}
```

### Before: Overly Compact

```typescript
const result = arr.filter(x => x > 0).map(x => x * 2).reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0);
```

### After: Clear Steps

```typescript
const positiveNumbers = arr.filter(x => x > 0);
const doubled = positiveNumbers.map(x => x * 2);
const sum = doubled.reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0);
```

### Before: Redundant Abstraction

```typescript
function isNotEmpty(arr: unknown[]): boolean {
  return arr.length > 0;
}

if (isNotEmpty(items)) {
  // ...
}
```

### After: Direct Check

```typescript
if (items.length > 0) {
  // ...
}
```

content.thinking:
The user is reconsidering the section title in Settings. Currently it's "Connect" — they're suggesting "Remote Access" might be better.

Let me think about this:
- "Connect" is vague — connect to what? It could mean connecting to a repo, an agent, etc.
- "Remote Access" is more specific and accurately describes what this feature does — accessing the desktop app from another device remotely
- The earlier design exploration tried to kill "remote access" as technical jargon, but actually in the context of a settings panel, it's the right level of specificity. The toggle label already says "Enable remote access" — so the section should match.
- Other products use similar naming: macOS uses "Remote Management", Chrome uses "Remote Desktop"


- Yeah, "Remote Access" is definitely the better choice here. "Connect" is too ambiguous for a settings section.

content.text: Yeah, "Remote Access" is better. "Connect" is too vague in a settings panel — connect to what? A repo? An agent? "Remote Access" immediately tells you what this section does. And it matches the toggle label ("Enable remote access") which already uses that term.

feat: redesign device pairing UX with two-word codes, QR dialog, and animated gate page

with smart paste handling. Adds a centered dialog with QR code hero for desktop pairing flow, and
redesigns the remote PairGatePage with three animated states (auto-connecting, success, manual entry).
Codes are now reusable within their 15-minute TTL for multi-device sharing.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
EOF
)"

input.command:
gh pr create --base main --title "feat: redesign device pairing UX with two-word codes and QR dialog" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
## Summary

- **Two-word pairing codes** (`SOFT TIGER` instead of `BEAR-4521`) — easier to dictate, type on mobile (no keyboard switch), and remember. Expanded word list from 100 to ~250 words (~62K combinations). Codes are reusable within their 15-minute TTL for multi-device sharing.
- **Single input field** on the remote pairing page — replaces the broken split WORD + NUMBER two-field input that prevented copy-paste. Smart paste handling extracts codes from full URLs.
- **QR code dialog** on desktop settings — centered dialog with QR hero (180px), eager code generation on open, Copy Link as primary action. Code state persists across dialog close/reopen. Added `qrcode.react` dependency.
- **Animated gate page** — three distinct states for the remote user: auto-connecting (pulsing dots, no form), success (spring checkmark, 1.5s hold), and manual entry (staggered entrance). Uses framer-motion with `prefers-reduced-motion` support.
- **Copy audit** — removed all technical jargon ("relay", "portal", "server ID", "pairing") from user-facing text. Gentle error messages throughout. Settings section renamed to "Remote Access".

## Test plan

- [x] Backend: 397 tests pass (code format, normalization, reuse within TTL)
- [ ] Desktop: open Settings > Remote Access > Connect a Device — verify QR code renders, code displays, Copy Link/Code work, countdown ticks, dialog reopen preserves code
- [ ] Phone: scan QR code — verify auto-connecting state, success checkmark, then workspace loads
- [ ] Browser: click shared link with `?pair=SOFT+TIGER` — verify auto-submit, success, navigation
- [ ] Manual: open pairing page without code in URL — verify form shows, paste handling works, error states

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)
EOF
)"

## Code Review: Redesign Device Pairing UX

**Commit:** `a763b02d0` — feat: redesign device pairing UX with two-word codes, QR dialog, and animated gate page

**Summary:** Replaces `WORD-NNNN` pairing codes with two-word codes (e.g. "SOFT TIGER"), consolidates split input into a single field with smart paste handling, adds a QR code dialog for desktop pairing, and redesigns the remote PairGatePage with three animated states. Codes are now reusable within their 15-minute TTL.

---

### Review Findings

I reviewed the diff across all 4 axes: CLAUDE.md compliance (2 passes), bug scan (diff-only), and introduced code analysis.

### **#1 — Hardcoded Tailwind color classes (CLAUDE.md violation)**

New code introduces `bg-emerald-500/10`, `text-emerald-500`, and `text-amber-500` — all hardcoded Tailwind color classes.

**CLAUDE.md rule (quoted):**

> **5. Use CSS Variables for Everything**
> - ❌ Never hardcode: `bg-blue-500`, `#3b82f6`, `rgba(59, 130, 246, 0.5)`
> - ✅ Always use tokens: `bg-primary`, `var(--primary)`

> **Use CSS variables for colors**
> - ❌ Don't hardcode colors: `text-green-500 dark:text-green-400`
> - ✅ Create semantic variables: `--color-status-success` → `text-[color:var(--status-success)]`

**Occurrences in the diff:**

| File | Line(s) | Class |
|------|---------|-------|
| `PairGatePage.tsx` | AnimatedCheckmark | `bg-emerald-500/10`, `text-emerald-500` |
| `AccessSection.tsx` | ConnectDeviceDialog success view | `bg-emerald-500/10`, `text-emerald-500` |
| `AccessSection.tsx` | Countdown warning | `text-amber-500` |

**Fix:** Define semantic CSS variables in `global.css` (e.g. `--color-status-success`, `--color-status-warning`) and use them via `bg-[var(--status-success)]/10` or similar token references.

---

### Items Reviewed and Validated as Correct

- **Two-word code generation** (`idx2` offset logic): Verified the Fisher-Yates-style uniform sampling ensures two different words are always selected.
- **Code normalization parity**: Backend `normalizePairCode` and frontend `normalizeCode` produce equivalent results despite slightly different ordering of `.trim()` vs `.replace()`.
- **Reusable codes security trade-off**: Intentional design decision documented in the commit message. Rate limiting (10 failures / 5-min lockout) and 15-minute TTL mitigate the expanded window.
- **Smart paste + auto-submit**: Correctly prevents default only when input is recognized; 300ms debounce avoids double-submission.
- **ConnectDeviceDialog success detection**: `prevDeviceCount` tracking via `useEffect` correctly handles all edge cases (device added while dialog closed, rapid successive pairings, cleanup of timers).
- **`handleToggle` no longer clears pair code state**: The entire section is gated by `enabled`, so orphaned state is inaccessible and naturally expires.
- **Framer Motion usage**: Follows CLAUDE.md guidance — AnimatePresence for mount/unmount, height:"auto" for expandable error message (documented as a valid Framer Motion use case), custom `EASE_OUT_QUART` curve used throughout.
- **Tests**: Comprehensive coverage for the new format, two-different-words invariant, all normalization variants (dashes, underscores, plus signs, mixed case, multiple spaces), and reuse-within-TTL semantics.
- **`qrcode.react` dependency**: Appropriate addition; `bg-white` on the QR container is intentional (QR codes require white background for scanning).

content.text:
Perfect! Now I have all the information I need. Let me provide a comprehensive report: --- ## Analysis Report: Hardcoded Color Usage vs Semantic Variables ### Summary **This IS a real violation of the codebase's design system, but it's currently MINIMAL and LIMITED to 3 files.** The codebase has a comprehensive semantic color system already defined, but only 10 instances of hardcoded colors exist, all in relatively new or isolated features. --- ### 1. Hardcoded Color Usage - COUNTS **Emerald colors: 8 instances** across 3 files - `apps/web/src/features/settings/ui/sections/AccessSection.tsx` - 4 instances - `apps/web/src/features/auth/ui/PairGatePage.tsx` - 2 instances - `apps/web/src/features/browser/automation/VisualEffects.stories.tsx` - 2 instances **Amber colors: 2 instances** across 1 file - `apps/web/src/features/settings/ui/sections/AccessSection.tsx` - 2 instances **Total: 10 instances across 3 files** — Very limited scope. --- ### 2. Semantic Color Variables ALREADY DEFINED **YES, comprehensive semantic colors ARE defined in `global.css`:** ```css --color-success: var(--success); /* oklch(0.53 0.16 155) */ --color-success-foreground: var(--success-foreground); --color-warning: var(--warning); /* oklch(0.53 0.14 75) */ --color-warning-foreground: var(--warning-foreground); --color-destructive: var(--destructive); /* oklch(0.53 0.19 25) */ --color-destructive-foreground: var(--destructive-foreground); --color-info: var(--info); --color-info-foreground: var(--info-foreground); ``` **Status-specific colors:** ```css --color-status-working: var(--status-working); /* Maps to success */ --color-status-working-fg: var(--status-working-fg); --color-status-unread: var(--status-unread); /* Maps to warning */ --color-status-unread-fg: var(--status-unread-fg); --color-status-done: var(--status-done); /* Maps to success */ ``` **Status indicators in workflow:** ```css --color-status-in-progress: var(--status-in-progress); /* = warning */ --color-status-in-review: oklch(0.53 0.16 300); /* purple */ --color-status-done: var(--status-done); /* = success */ ``` --- ### 3. Semantic Color Values (Light Mode) From `global.css`: ```css /* Light mode */ --success: oklch(0.53 0.16 155); /* Green at L=0.53 */ --success-foreground: #ffffff; --warning: oklch(0.53 0.14 75); /* Yellow/Amber at L=0.53 */ --warning-foreground: #ffffff; --destructive: oklch(0.53 0.19 25); /* Red at L=0.53 */ --destructive-foreground: #ffffff; ``` **Dark mode:** ```css --success: oklch(0.68 0.12 150); /* Green at L=0.68, desaturated */ --success-foreground: #0b0b0b; --warning: oklch(0.68 0.13 75); /* Yellow/Amber at L=0.68, desaturated */ --warning-foreground: #0b0b0b; --destructive: oklch(0.65 0.13 20); /* Red at L=0.65, desaturated */ --destructive-foreground: oklch(0.86 0 0); ``` --- ### 4. What the Hardcoded Colors Are Actually Used For **Emerald (success/positive states):** - Connection status indicator (connected = green dot) - Success checkmark icon background and icon - "Device Connected" success state animation **Amber (warning/expiring states):** - Code expiration countdown warning - "Setting up..." status indicator (neutral/in-progress) --- ### 5. Why This Is a Real (But Minor) Violation The CLAUDE.md specifies: > Never hardcode colors at all costs > ✅ Always use tokens: `bg-primary`, `var(--primary)`, `color-mix(...)` The hardcoded `emerald-500`, `emerald-400`, `amber-500`, `amber-400` bypass the theme system and won't adapt if the design system colors change. The semantic variables `--success` and `--warning` are designed to flip between light/dark modes, but these hardcoded colors don't participate in that system. --- ### 6. Should You Fix This? **YES, but it's LOW priority:** | Aspect | Status | |--------|--------| | **Scale** | Minimal (10 instances, 3 files) | | **Impact** | Low (isolated to auth + settings features) | | **Maintainability** | Medium (if colors ever change, these won't adapt) | | **Design System Compliance** | Clear violation | | **Theme Switching** | Not tested for dark mode behavior | **Fix approach:** Replace all `emerald-*` with Tailwind `bg-success`, `text-success` (uses `--color-success` from global.css) Replace all `amber-*` with Tailwind `bg-warning`, `text-warning` (uses `--color-warning` from global.css) **Example replacement:** ```tsx // Before
// After
``` --- ### 7. Design System Completeness The codebase already has: - **Semantic colors defined** (success, warning, destructive, info) - **Theme-aware CSS variables** (light/dark mode) - **Status colors** (working, compacting, unread, in-progress, in-review, done) - **Foreground colors** for contrast - **Surface variants** (tints, surfaces for each semantic color) - **OKLCH color space** for perceptually uniform theming This is a **mature, well-designed color system**. The 10 hardcoded instances are simply oversights in newer features. --- ### Conclusion **This is NOT a systemic problem** — it's a handful of oversights in 3 relatively new files (auth/settings features + stories). The codebase has a comprehensive semantic color system already in place. Fixing this would be straightforward (search/replace 10 instances) and would bring full compliance with CLAUDE.md's design system rules.

content.text:
**Review verdict: 1 actionable finding, now fixed.**

The review identified hardcoded `emerald-500`/`amber-500` Tailwind color classes violating the CLAUDE.md rule "never hardcode colors." The codebase already has semantic tokens (`--success`, `--warning`, `--destructive`) defined in `global.css` with proper OKLCH light/dark mode values. Replaced all 10 instances:

| Before | After |
|---|---|
| `bg-emerald-500/10` | `bg-success/10` |
| `text-emerald-500` | `text-success` |
| `text-amber-500` | `text-warning` |
| `bg-amber-400` | `bg-warning` |
| `bg-emerald-500` (status dot) | `bg-success` |
| `bg-red-400` (status dot) | `bg-destructive/60` |

Everything else the review validated as correct — the code generation logic, normalization parity, security trade-offs, animation patterns, and test coverage are all sound. Pushed to the PR.

content.text: Two failures in the integration test — it still expects the old `WORD-NNNN` format and one-time-use behavior. Let me read the test:

Two failures:

1. **Line 174**: `expect(code).toMatch(/^[A-Z]+-\d{4}$/)` — expects old `WORD-NNNN` format, but we now generate `WORD WORD`
2. **Line 211-227**: "rejects a reused pairing code" — expects 401 on second use, but codes are now reusable within TTL

Also the error message on line 226 checks for the old text `"Invalid or expired pairing code"` vs our new `"Invalid or expired connection code"`.

content.text:
Fixed and pushed. The two CI failures were:

1. **`generates a WORD-NNNN code from localhost`** — regex `/^[A-Z]+-\d{4}$/` didn't match the new two-word format `CURL AGED`. Updated to `/^[A-Z]+ [A-Z]+$/`.

2. **`rejects a reused pairing code`** — expected 401 on second use, but codes are now intentionally reusable within TTL. Replaced with a test that verifies two devices can pair with the same code.
